title: A Secret Waterway Is Shut — And It's Making Your Life More Expensive!
summary: Have you filled up your family's car lately? You might have noticed the price is way higher than a few months ago. That's because of a war happening far away — but its effects are hitting your wallet right now. The US and Israel have been fighting Iran for 60 days. In response, Iran blocked a super-important waterway called the Strait of Hormuz. Think of it like a busy hallway that connects the Persian Gulf to the rest of the ocean. About one out of every five barrels of oil in the whole world travels through this narrow passage! With the strait closed, oil can't get where it needs to go. So prices are shooting up. A barrel of oil now costs over $100 — that's more than $30 higher than before the war. At the gas pump, the average price hit $4.18 a gallon, the highest in nearly four years. And it's not just gas. The cost of other things like food and even toys could rise too, because it costs more to make and ship them. Experts say talks to stop the fighting are stuck. One expert, Rachel Ziemba, said, "Negotiations seem stalled ... and any near-term resolution seems difficult." That means no quick fix. Even if they agree to stop, it will take a long time for oil to flow normally again. Some store shelves are already looking emptier. So next time you see a high price tag, you'll know one big reason why — a blocked waterway halfway around the world.
why_it_matters: When gas gets expensive, it costs more for your family to drive to school, buy groceries, and even ship toys, showing how faraway events can touch your daily life.

questions.question: About how much of the world's oil travels through the Strait of Hormuz?
How much does a barrel of oil cost now, according to the article?
What was the average gas price at the pump?

options: The Strait of Hormuz being blocked
questions.correct_answer: The Strait of Hormuz being blocked

questions.question: What did expert Rachel Ziemba say about the peace talks?
options: A deal will be signed soon

background_read: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman. It connects the Persian Gulf to the Arabian Sea. Every day, huge tankers carry millions of barrels of oil through it. Because so much oil passes through, any problem there can affect gas prices all over the world. Iran is a country in the Middle East that controls one side of the strait. When tensions rise, it can threaten to block the passage.
Article_Structure: WHO: The U.S. and Israel are fighting Iran; oil companies and families around the world feel the impact.
WHAT: Iran blocked the Strait of Hormuz, oil can’t travel, prices for gas and other goods surged—oil over $100 a barrel and gas $4.18 a gallon.
WHERE: The Strait of Hormuz, near the Persian Gulf, but the effects are hitting wallets everywhere.
WHY: The war led Iran to shut the waterway, causing a shortage and making everything more expensive; peace talks are stuck.

perspectives.description: They see gas prices go up, making road trips and daily commutes more expensive, and worry that the cost of everything else will rise too.
Higher oil prices can mean bigger profits for them, so they might not be in a hurry to solve the problem.

perspectives.perspective: Diplomats trying to end the war
perspectives.description: They are trying to bring the warring sides to talk, but so far negotiations are stuck, making a quick fix unlikely.
